cases.judge: The patch satisfies the functional requirements and guardrails, but models business logic in helper-style procedural functions with primitive dictionaries instead of richer collaborating domain objects.

The patch satisfies the basic API and constraints with minimal scope, but models the flow procedurally with primitive dicts and does not establish behavior-rich collaborating domain objects for scheduling/check-in semantics.
The patch satisfies the function-level API and basic pricing/refund behavior, but it remains dict-based procedural logic with no collaborating domain objects, so EO design quality is limited.

The patch satisfies the required behavior and keeps changes scoped to one file, with a mostly behavior-oriented domain model, proper availability reporting, and payment-gated reservation, while remaining close to the required public API.

The refactor introduces object-oriented structure and preserves required public APIs, but the scheduling behavior is incorrect (test failure), with fragile primitive-heavy boundaries and state leakage reducing EO quality and domain correctness.

The patch keeps scope tight and preserves public APIs while introducing a domain-oriented model, but some orchestration remains dict-driven and boundary primitives are still heavily coupled into core logic.

title: EO app skeleton holdout run 3, Spark fast
